message.content: {"top_matches":[{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.5},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.3},{"player":"DOG","probability":0.2}],"behavioral_basis":["These three players contributed in most rounds (5/6) — consistent with a conditional-cooperator strategy I would likely follow: generally contribute to support the group but sometimes withhold if cooperation is low or uncertain.","Their single defections occurred in rounds where several others also defected, suggesting responsiveness to peers rather than pure free-riding; I would behave similarly (cooperate by default, reduce contributions when others do).","None of them are extreme unconditional cooperators or persistent free-riders, matching my tendency to balance social preferences and self-interest across repeated rounds."],"uncertainty_notes":["I only see contributions and one chat message; motives (intentional reciprocity, errors, or experimentation) are unclear.","Group-level dynamics and private payoffs may have influenced individual choices in ways not visible here.","Several players have very similar patterns (e.g., SLOTH, CHICKEN, RABBIT), so other nearby matches are plausible given limited information."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.6},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.3},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["SLOTH: started as a full contributor (20) for several rounds then cut contribution sharply after repeated low contributions from another player — this matches a conditional-cooperator who retaliates by withdrawing cooperation when others free-ride, which is how I would plausibly behave.","GORILLA: always contributed the maximum (20) every round, consistent with an unconditional cooperator or ‘saint’ who values group payoff over short-term gain — I could behave this way but less likely than conditional cooperation.","DUCK: contributed below the maximum throughout and even punished a full contributor once, suggesting inconsistent or noisy strategy and possibly spiteful or mistaken punishment; this is the least like how I would act."],"uncertainty_notes":["Motivations behind punishment and the timing of contribution changes are not observable (e.g., messages, intentions), so inferred strategy labels (conditional cooperator, unconditional cooperator, spiteful/noisy) are tentative.","Small number of rounds and only three observed players limit confidence; different hidden factors (risk preferences, misunderstanding, retaliation) could explain the same actions.","Punishment is rare in the trace (one 1-unit punish by DUCK) so its interpretation (targeting, error, message) is particularly uncertain."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.65},{"player":"CHICK","probability":0.30},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistently contributed the full 20 each round (strong pro-cooperation stance).","Regularly rewarded other players—especially contributors—indicating willingness to reinforce cooperative behavior.","Used chat to encourage contribution and explain the benefits of cooperation (prosocial leadership).","CHICK similarly contributed regularly and systematically rewarded contributors, matching a conditional-reward strategy I would use.","SLOTH contributed often and called out non-contributors, which aligns somewhat with my tendency to both cooperate and publicly advocate for cooperation."],"uncertainty_notes":["Observations are limited to revealed contributions, rewards, and chat — internal motives and off-record decision rules are unknown.","Some players (e.g., SNAKE, CROCODILE) show mixed behavior later in the game that could reflect strategic shifts I might or might not adopt.","I may behave slightly differently depending on group dynamics or if I could target punishments/rewards more precisely; the mapping above reflects the most typical steady cooperative strategy I would plausibly follow."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.6},{"player":"SNAKE","probability":0.25},{"player":"CHICK","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["SLOTH: Consistently contributes 20, posts pro-cooperation chat messages, and applies small, targeted punishments and occasional small rewards — matches a cooperative, norm-enforcing style I would use.","SNAKE: Frequently advocates contributing, punishes non‑contributors repeatedly (moderate, targeted punishments), and sometimes rewards cooperators — fits a conditional cooperator who enforces norms.","CHICK: Actively rewards many players and encourages others in chat and later contributes regularly — fits a supportive cooperator who uses positive reinforcement rather than harsh sanctions."],"uncertainty_notes":["I cannot see players' exact motivations (some punishments may be retaliatory or spiteful rather than norm enforcement).","Contribution history and messaging sometimes conflict (players who chat pro‑cooperation sometimes failed to contribute early).","Punishment magnitudes vary widely across players (e.g., OWL’s very large deductions) so matching is based on typical rather than extreme rounds."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.6},{"player":"PARROT","probability":0.25},{"player":"MONKEY","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistently cooperative (mostly contributed the maximum once norm established).","Used positive rewards to acknowledge and reinforce cooperation (Gorilla rewarded others, including broad distribution in round 16).","Encouraging and team-oriented chat: supportive messages and morale-building (Parrot, Monkey).","Forgiving/tolerant of occasional mistakes while emphasizing group payoff rather than punitive retaliation."],"uncertainty_notes":["We only observe revealed contributions, chat, and sparse reward actions — underlying motives are ambiguous.","Reward use is visible only in a few rounds; some players with similar contribution patterns did not use rewards, so matching is imperfect.","Player behavior could vary under different stakes or if identities/roles changed, so alignment is tentative."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.62},{"player":"DOG","probability":0.25},{"player":"PARROT","probability":0.13}],"behavioral_basis":["CHICKEN: consistently high contributions, frequent rewarding of others, and active agreement/praise—signals cooperative leadership and reciprocity, which matches how I would play.","DOG: mostly high contributions, agreed to the group plan, and occasional rewarding—reflects steady cooperation and moderate enforcement, similar to my likely style.","PARROT: initiated coordination to contribute fully in the final rounds and used chat to promote cooperation, showing proactive coordination even if contributions were variable earlier."],"uncertainty_notes":["I only observe revealed contributions, rewards, and chat; internal motives and off-record considerations are unknown.","Some players show mixed behavior (e.g., early free-riding then later cooperation), so alignment is based on overall tendency rather than perfect consistency.","Reward allocations are visible but not recipients' identities in summaries, which limits inference about conditional reciprocity strategies."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.6},{"player":"PARROT","probability":0.25},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent contribution: SLOTH contributed the full amount in every round, matching my preference to cooperate regularly.","Targeted rewarding: SLOTH and CHICKEN both reward others fairly often and sometimes invest multiple reward units in specific players (e.g., SLOTH giving 2–3 units), which fits my tendency to selectively reward helpful/important behavior rather than reward everyone indiscriminately.","Pro-social but not indiscriminate: PARROT contributes almost always and frequently rewards others, showing a generally cooperative, reciprocity-focused style that matches my likely approach (cooperate most rounds and reciprocate/encourage cooperation)."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only contributions and anonymous reward actions are observable — motives (e.g., signaling, experimentation) are ambiguous.","Some players (e.g., COW, SNAKE, OWL) give many small rewards to almost everyone or defect often; these patterns could reflect different but plausible strategies I might adopt in other contexts, so assignment is probabilistic.","Behaviors changed across rounds (especially in late rounds), so the match is based on overall tendency rather than exact round-by-round identity."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"CROCODILE","probability":0.55},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.3},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["CROCODILE: consistently contributed and used both rewards and occasional punishments across many rounds (e.g., rewarded CHICK in R1, punished DOG/PARROT/MOOSE in R3, rewarded many players in R14) — matches a strategy of steady contribution plus targeted use of rewards/punishments to sustain cooperation.","GORILLA: frequently rewarded apparent cooperators across early and middle rounds (R1,R2,R3,R4,R5), indicating a cooperative, reinforcement-focused strategy even after a single defection (R2). This fits a conditional-cooperator who emphasizes positive reinforcement.","SLOTH: contributed every round and repeatedly punished clear defectors (e.g., punished CHICK in R1 and R7, punished GORILLA in R2), reflecting a consistent contributing player who uses punishment to discourage free-riding."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only observed actions (contributions and punishment/reward units) are available; motives (e.g., spite, reciprocity, mistakes) are ambiguous.","Some players had occasional one-off defections that could be errors rather than strategic choices (e.g., GORILLA in R2), making behavioral inference noisy.","Anonymity and lack of direct messages limit ability to infer social targeting or alliances; my self-match is based on typical conditional-cooperator behavior (contribute each round, reward cooperators, punish defectors modestly)."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"CHICK","probability":0.6},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.25},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent full contributions across nearly all rounds (strong cooperative strategy).","Frequent encouraging/chatty messages urging others to contribute and calling out defections—matches my tendency to both cooperate and promote cooperation.","Quick forgiveness/understanding of apparent mistakes (e.g., apologies for misclicks) and steady return to cooperation—aligned with a cooperative but pragmatic approach."],"uncertainty_notes":["Observed behavior may hide accidental misclicks vs intentional free-riding.","Chat messages and contributions are summary-level; motivations (strategic signaling, boredom, payoff focus) are not directly observable.","Players’ private payoffs and any off-platform coordination are unknown; similarity is judged only on the revealed actions and chat."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"PARROT","probability":0.6},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.25},{"player":"MOOSE","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistently contributed each round (reliable cooperator), encouraged group cooperation in chat — aligns with my tendency to cooperate and promote coordination (PARROT).","Avoided aggressive moralizing about punishment and expressed willingness to keep group norms by occasional targeted punishment — matches my preference for measured enforcement rather than frequent costly retaliation.","CHICKEN matches my social, encouraging style and reluctance to punish; MOOSE matches a stricter enforcer profile I might adopt only occasionally if free-riding persisted."],"uncertainty_notes":["Inferring internal motives from observed actions/chat is imprecise; multiple players show overlapping cooperative behaviors.","My own behavior could shift depending on how costly I perceive punishment and whether defections seem accidental vs. intentional, changing similarity to PARROT vs. MOOSE.","Some players (e.g., GORILLA, RABBIT) are low-chat cooperators; if I preferred quiet cooperation over chatty encouragement, my match ranking could change."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.6},{"player":"CROCODILE","probability":0.3},{"player":"COW","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistently contributes (always 20) — I would cooperate rather than free-ride in this public-goods setting.","Regular, targeted punishment of non-contributors: GORILLA punishes many defectors with modest (mostly single-unit) sanctions, matching my tendency to punish reliably but not excessively.","CROCODILE is also a steady cooperator who punishes repeat defectors with somewhat stronger (multi-unit) sanctions — this matches a plausible variant of my strategy (willing to pay more to deter persistent free-riders).","COW is a consistent cooperator who punishes occasionally and conservatively, matching a cautious pro-social punisher profile I might adopt in some rounds."],"uncertainty_notes":["I only observe summary actions, not motives or deliberation — identical actions could stem from different preferences (inequity aversion, spite, norm enforcement).","Punishment identities are anonymous to targets and total rounds were unknown to players; those framing details might change how I would actually behave.","Some players occasionally use very large or erratic punishments (e.g., WHALE, DOG) — without context it's hard to know whether I would match those extreme patterns, so I favored moderate enforcers."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.75},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.15},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistently cooperative after an initial interface mistake and actively encouraged group cooperation (matches my inclination to cooperate and communicate).","Used chat to explain errors, persuade others, and foster teamwork — I would likely do the same to promote the group-optimal outcome.","CHICKEN’s steady cooperation with minimal chat matches a quieter cooperative style I might also show in some sessions.","GORILLA’s consistent cooperation but more procedural questioning reflects a cooperative-but-curious stance I sometimes take."],"uncertainty_notes":["Public messages can be strategic or insincere; chat content may not fully reveal true motivations.","I may behave differently depending on mood or perceived reliability of others (more silent like CHICKEN or more active like DUCK).","The observed players’ early actions (e.g., accidental claims) could reflect interface errors rather than true preferences, adding uncertainty to alignment."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.65},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.25},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["I would mostly withdraw (defect) each round but sometimes contribute fully to encourage cooperation or reciprocate — GORILLA shows a similar mixed pattern (several defections with occasional contributions).","I tend to be social/communicative in experiments; GORILLA sent friendly chat messages, which matches my likelihood of greeting/engaging.","I would be unlikely to use costly punishment given no strong signals of cooperation enforcement; no player used punishment, so non-punishing cooperators/defectors align with my likely behavior."],"uncertainty_notes":["Observed actions (contribute 0 or 20, chat) do not reveal motivations (experimentation, mistakes, or strategic signalling).","No punishments were used, so we cannot infer players' enforcement preferences or responses to norm violations.","Chat appearances are sparse; absence of chat doesn't mean lack of social intent — players may behave similarly but not type messages."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.6},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.3},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["SLOTH: Consistently moderate contributions (often ~10) with little use of costly rewards — matches a conditional cooperator who prefers contributing but rarely pays to reward/punish.","GORILLA: Frequently high contributions and the most active rewarder — matches a pro-social, enforcement-oriented player who both contributes and uses rewards to shape others' behavior.","CHICKEN: Fluctuating between 0/20 contributions and selectively rewarding (often reciprocating GORILLA) — matches a conditional reciprocator but less consistent than the top matches."],"uncertainty_notes":["Behavioral motives (e.g., spite, experiment strategy, mistakes) are not directly observable from contributions and rewards alone.","Reward targets sometimes include low contributors, so apparent generosity might reflect attempts to build alliances rather than pure rewarding of cooperators.","Later-round shifts (many zeros late in the game) could reflect end-game effects that would alter how closely any player matches my steady strategy."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.6},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.3},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Mostly high contributions with occasional downward adjustments matches my likely mix of cooperation and payoff-safeguarding — GORILLA contributes 20 most rounds but drops to 10 sometimes, suggesting conditional cooperation.","SLOTH is a strong unconditional cooperator (almost always 20) which is plausible if I prioritized group payoff over personal safety, so I assign secondary weight to that pattern.","DUCK's highly variable and frequently low contributions look like a freeriding or erratic strategy, which is least like how I would behave; I might occasionally defect but not that erratically."],"uncertainty_notes":["I cannot see players' intentions or their private reasoning; observed patterns could reflect mistakes, experimentation, or strategic responses to unobserved factors.","No punishments occurred, so there is no signal about norm enforcement preferences — punishment behavior would change how closely I match a player.","As a participant I wouldn’t see contemporaneous choices before acting; inferred strategies are based only on aggregate observed contributions and may misattribute conditional strategies."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.65},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.25},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["DUCK: Mostly high contributions (20 in multiple rounds, generally >10) and no use of costly rewards — matches a straightforward cooperative strategy I would use (contribute strongly to the public pot rather than spend on signaling).","GORILLA: Mid-level contributions (around 10) with occasional small, targeted rewards to others — matches a conditional/coordinator style I might use if I wanted to nudge cooperative behavior without always giving maximum contribution.","SLOTH: Began with full contributions then declined over rounds — consistent with a conditional cooperator who reduces giving after perceived free-riding. This is a possible but less preferred match for my typical behavior, which is more consistently cooperative or strategically using small rewards."],"uncertainty_notes":["I must infer motives only from contribution and reward amounts; underlying intentions (strategic vs. altruistic) are unknown.","Small number of reward actions (only two 1-unit rewards) limits ability to identify a rewarding strategy robustly.","My stated match assumes a preference for steady cooperation; if my real preference were punitive or highly opportunistic, alignment probabilities would change."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.6},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.25},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistently high contributions (mostly 15→20) indicating a reliable cooperator, combined with occasional small rewards to other cooperators — aligns with a cooperative, reciprocity-focused strategy I would use (SLOTH).","Frequent, proactive rewarding of many players (including multi-unit rewards) and generally high contributions — matches a strong norm-enforcer / incentivizer I might adopt if I prioritized promoting cooperation broadly (GORILLA).","Generally moderate-to-high contributions with some modest reciprocal rewards; less extreme than the top matches but still cooperative and supportive (DUCK)."],"uncertainty_notes":["Player motives are inferred from actions only; rewards could reflect reputation-building, experimentation, or mistakes rather than pure pro-social motives.","Some players (e.g., GORILLA) show occasional low contributions that could be strategic or accidental; without private reasoning or messages the mapping is noisy.","Behavior could evolve over unobserved rounds or be context-dependent; my self-match reflects typical tendencies rather than certainty about any single round."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.6},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.3},{"player":"PARROT","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Prefer to contribute fully most rounds but use targeted punishment to deter clear free-riding (matches DUCK's frequent 20s plus two single-unit punishments of defection).","Also likely to encourage cooperation verbally and call out defectors rather than relying only on punishment (matches GORILLA's consistent 20s plus persuasive chat).","Would accept being a quiet consistent contributor if not using chat/punishment often (matches PARROT's steady 20s with no messages or sanctions)."],"uncertainty_notes":["Can't observe private motives—punishments/rewards and small contribution deviations might be mistakes (misclicks) rather than strategic choices.","Punishment magnitude and timing choices are sparsely observed (only two punish events), so inference about enforcement style has limited evidence.","No information on players' responses to repeated defectors beyond a few instances, so long-run enforcement tendencies are uncertain."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.6},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.25},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["Regularly contributes (cooperates) in most rounds while also using rewards—shows conditional cooperation plus active rewarding, matching my inclination to both contribute and positively reinforce cooperators (DUCK).","Frequently rewards many players (including cooperators) and mixes cooperation and defection, suggesting a strategic, reciprocating approach rather than blind altruism—consistent with how I might behave if balancing payoff and norm enforcement (GORILLA).","Almost always contributes (high cooperation rate) but does not appear to use rewards; aligns with my baseline preference to cooperate, though I would likely also send rewards, making SLOTH a partial match."],"uncertainty_notes":["Limited number of rounds and no information about players' private motives—actions could be driven by experiment-specific strategies rather than stable preferences.","Reward sizes and timing vary across rounds; inferring intentions (punishment vs. reward, reciprocity vs. generalized giving) is uncertain.","Chat messages are sparse and may not reflect decision rules; behavioral matching emphasizes revealed contributions and reward patterns only."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.75},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.20},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent full contributions across all rounds (strong cooperators) — DUCK and SLOTH always contributed 20.","Proactive promotion of cooperation (DUCK sent chat urging maximum contribution and later asked CHICKEN to give).","Generous, targeted rewarding of cooperators and withholding rewards from defectors (DUCK consistently rewarded cooperators and did not reward observed defectors).","SLOTH also consistently cooperated and rewarded others but was less vocal and slightly less active than DUCK.","GORILLA shows mixed behavior (one defection, irregular rewarding), making it a poorer match."],"uncertainty_notes":["Cannot observe unexpressed motives—rewards could reflect reputation management rather than pure altruism.","Small group (4 players) and only 6 rounds limit inference; occasional rewards to defectors (by others) complicate strict classification.","Players’ private payoffs and exact timing of reward decisions are not visible, so some behavior could be reactive to unseen details."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.75},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.2},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent cooperation every round (would cooperate each round).","Regular use of small punishments for defectors combined with frequent small rewards for cooperators — aligns with a preference for moderate enforcement plus positive reinforcement (seen in GORILLA).","SLOTH’s pattern of cooperating and occasionally imposing stronger punishments partly matches my willingness to sanction defectors, but SLOTH’s harsher punishments are less like my typical moderate style.","CHICKEN’s single defection among mostly cooperative moves makes them a weak match (cooperative but opportunistic)."],"uncertainty_notes":["Cannot observe players’ private motivations or message content — punishments could be emotional retaliation or strategic enforcement.","Unknown whether repeated rewards reflect a stable strategy or reaction to earlier rounds — matching is based only on observed actions, not underlying intent.","Only four players and six rounds observed; limited sample may over- or under-emphasize tendencies (e.g., SLOTH’s heavy punishment could be an outlier)."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"MOOSE","probability":0.6},{"player":"RABBIT","probability":0.25},{"player":"OWL","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["MOOSE: contributed moderately early and then consistently full (or high) contributions later, and used small, targeted punishments against low contributors — matches a conditional-cooperator who enforces norms without excessive retaliation.","RABBIT: ramped up contributions over rounds and punished with small units at times, indicating selective enforcement and a tendency toward cooperation rather than vindictiveness.","OWL: contribution pattern is generally cooperative with occasional small punishments; less active as a punisher but consistent with a cooperative, reserved enforcer."],"uncertainty_notes":["I only observe contributions and punishment actions (who punished whom and unit sizes) but not players' private summaries or motives, so behavioral inferences are tentative.","Some actions (e.g., very large punishments like DOG->DUCK of 21 units) could be misclicks, mistakes, or atypical outliers, which complicates matching.","Players' strategy may also have been contingent on private information or beliefs about others; my match is based on overall patterns (contribution trajectory + punishment intensity/type)."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"DOG","probability":0.6},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.3},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["DOG explicitly initiated group coordination and consistently contributed the maximum after encouraging others — matches my tendency to propose clear cooperative strategies and follow through.","GORILLA was social, pragmatic, and slightly cautious (low contribution in round 1) but quickly committed and stayed engaged — matches my tendency to be cooperative but ask practical questions and be chatty.","SLOTH was low-key, supportive, and steady in messaging — matches my quieter cooperative style when I prefer minimal but positive interaction."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only public chat and contributions are observable; private motives and exact reasoning are unknown.","Early-round deviations by some players (e.g., low first-round contributions) could reflect mistakes, testing, or interface issues, which complicates precise alignment.","Other players (e.g., DUCK, RABBIT) showed similar cooperative behavior at times and could also be plausible matches given limited behavioral signals."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.5},{"player":"PARROT","probability":0.3},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.2}],"behavioral_basis":["Actively advocated the group strategy (asked to go \"all in\") and consistently contributed the full 20 after coordination — matches my tendency to promote and follow collective, cooperative strategies.","Parrot similarly encouraged the group, participated in chat, and moved to full contributions quickly — aligns with my tendency to be communicative and cooperative but slightly less central than Duck.","Gorilla was cooperative in contributions and supportive in chat but more quiet — matches my fallback cooperative stance when I prefer contribution over active leadership or punishment."],"uncertainty_notes":["I only observe public contributions, punishments, and chat; private motives or off-chat reasoning are unknown.","Some players briefly defected early then switched to full cooperation (e.g., SLOTH); my real behavior might match those conditional cooperators depending on perceived norm enforcement.","Punishment behavior could be consistent with strong norm enforcement I might do, but I often prefer persuasion/coordination over costly punishment, so I de-emphasized punishers (e.g., OWL, MOOSE) in my top matches."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.6},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.25},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent full contributions: CHICKEN and DUCK contributed 20 in both rounds, matching my tendency to cooperate by contributing the full endowment.","Pro-social communication: CHICKEN explicitly encouraged full contributions, which matches my likelihood to persuade/coordinate others verbally.","Use of incentives: GORILLA rewarded cooperators early and pushed for full contributions despite an initial lower contribution, reflecting a mix of signaling and targeted rewards I might use if trying to shift group norms."],"uncertainty_notes":["Labels and limited rounds: two rounds provide a small behavioral sample — motives (strategic, reputational, or sincere) are ambiguous.","Unobserved private motives: players’ private calculations or intended long‑term strategy (e.g., planned retaliation or signaling) aren’t visible.","Punish/reward choices limited: absence of punishment by some players could reflect budget, preference, or oversight; I might have used rewards or mild punishment depending on context."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"COW","probability":0.75},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.15},{"player":"PARROT","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["COW: Always contributes the full 20 each round and frequently gives rewards to many peers — matches a stable, strongly pro-social strategy (consistent high contribution + active positive reciprocity).","GORILLA: Contributes 20 almost every round but rarely (if ever) appears as a reward source — matches a reliable cooperator who focuses on contributing rather than social signaling.","PARROT: Regularly contributes moderate-to-high amounts (mostly 10–20) and occasionally rewards others; represents a steady, conditional cooperator who also uses rewards selectively."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only nine rounds observed — behavior could drift later (e.g., some players switch from high to low contributions).","Rewards are anonymous to recipients here, so motives (punishment vs. praise, reciprocal targeting) are inferred indirectly and may be misinterpreted.","I assume I would both contribute consistently and use rewards to reinforce cooperation; if I were less inclined to pay reward costs, my match might shift toward GORILLA instead."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"OWL","probability":0.6},{"player":"MONKEY","probability":0.25},{"player":"MOOSE","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["OWL consistently contributes at or near the maximum and repeatedly issues small rewards to many players each round — indicating a steady, pro-social strategy of high contribution plus positive reinforcement, which matches my default preference to cooperate and encourage others.","MONKEY shifts from modest to high contributions and selectively rewards cooperators while occasionally punishing defectors — a conditional-cooperator style (reward cooperators, deter free-riders) that I might plausibly use if I monitor and respond to others.","MOOSE contributes moderately-to-high and frequently punishes obvious defectors (e.g., PARROT, CROCODILE) while sometimes rewarding cooperators — an enforcement-oriented cooperative approach I might use to sustain group cooperation if I felt norm enforcement was needed."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only nine rounds observed — limited data to fully characterize long-run strategies.","Players' identities and exact motives are not visible (e.g., whether punishments were reactive or premeditated), so matching is approximate.","Other players (e.g., PINGUIN as an unconditional cooperator or COW as another enforcer) could also resemble my behavior depending on whether I prioritized unconditional cooperation vs. active norm enforcement."]}
